parts.title: Transcript
parts.annotated_transcript_html:
Speaker 1: You now have some time to look at the questions on your screen. Now answer the questions on your screen.

Speaker 1: Good afternoon, class. In today's lecture, we're going to be looking at one of the foundational documents in the history of human rights, Magna Carta. Let's start by looking at the story behind its creation.

Speaker 1: Magna Carta just means Great Charter in Latin, and this great charter, or great document, was originally signed to end an armed conflict between King John and some of the most powerful landowners of England.

Speaker 1: As is so often the case, the argument between the parties was over money. Let's look at this in more detail.

Speaker 1: When John came to the throne, large parts of northern France had belonged to the English crown, but by 1204, John had lost most of those.

Speaker 1: In the following 10 years, he taxed the landowners more and more in a series of unsuccessful military attempts to win those lands back.

Speaker 1: Then in 1214, John's army was completely defeated by the French, and he was forced to pay the massive compensation, leading to yet more taxes back in England.

Speaker 1: The landowners would forgive neither the taxes nor the military failures. The following year, they rebelled against bad King John, as they called him, and took control of the cities of London, Lincoln, and Exeter.

Speaker 1: Their actions received a great deal of support, and King John was left with no choice but to try to make peace.

Speaker 1: It was thus agreed that John and the landowners would meet on neutral territory to sign the peace agreement, which later came to be known as Magna Carta.
